critique.summary: The article argues that individuals and businesses should maintain their own websites rather than relying solely on social media platforms. It highlights the risks of platform dependency, such as loss of control over content and audience, and advocates for owning digital presence through websites and mailing lists.
mainPoints: Social media platforms are easy and cheap but do not offer ownership or control over content and followers.
Having a personal or business website provides stability and control over information like rates and hours.
Social media platforms can change rules or remove accounts without recourse, risking loss of audience and content.
Email mailing lists are recommended as a reliable way to maintain contact with audiences independent of social media.
The internet was originally built on interconnected websites, and reliance on walled social media platforms is a recent and problematic development.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about the risks of relying solely on social media platforms are accurate and supported by well-documented cases, such as Twitter's changes and account removals. It is true that users do not own their content or follower counts on platforms like Facebook and Instagram, and that websites provide more control and permanence. The recommendation to maintain a mailing list is also widely accepted as best practice for direct audience engagement. However, the article is opinionated and lacks citations or multiple perspectives, and some language is informal and emotionally charged.
considerations: The article does not address the technical and financial barriers some individuals or small businesses might face when creating and maintaining a website.
It overlooks the marketing and engagement advantages that social media platforms can offer, such as discoverability and network effects.
The article does not mention hybrid approaches that combine websites with social media presence for broader reach.
Security and privacy concerns related to managing one's own website and mailing list are not discussed.
The article's tone and language may alienate some readers and reduce its persuasive effectiveness.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the challenges and importance of small businesses having their own websites in 2026. While many agree that having a website is beneficial for control and permanence, commenters highlight the technical complexity, maintenance burden, and the convenience of social media and platform-based presences as reasons why many businesses avoid traditional websites.

comments.author: Arainach
comments.text:
Someone wrote and deleted a comment saying

> I don't get it. LLMs are supposed to have 100% bridged this gap from "normie" to "DIY website." What's missing?

This is an all too common thought process among technologists, so:

Where to even start? Well, let's start that every single "AI" company is massively overhyping everything to try to avoid any unfortunate realizations about the emperor's clothes regarding their CapEx and finances. Yes, even your favorite one.

The very short version: running a small business like a restaraunt takes all your resources and then 20% more. Long hours, hard work, all your time. You do not have 2 hours to learn about LLMs or to pick which company to pay. From there:

* Most people don't know what they want

* Most people don't know the words for what they want

* Even if you say "I want a website", what do you want it do look like? To say? These people aren't experts in web UX nor should they be.

* You have some HTML and images. Where do they go now? Again people literally don't know what they want or need. If you realize you need a "web host", how do you pick a trustworthy one? How do you know if it's a good price? How do you get a domain name? How do you get the files onto the server?

* Do you want people to be able to buy things? Now you're taking payment methods and have security concerns.

* Your site is live. You want to change something on it. How do you do that? Where are the original files? How do you change them? How do you get the changes on the server?

It's not "Hey, write me a website". There are lots of steps that assume a lot of knowledge, and it is easier, faster, and better for people to focus on their expertise and just pay some service for their web shop.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and realistic explanation of the practical barriers small business owners face, making it a highly accurate and insightful contribution.

comments.author: Lammy
comments.text:
> Set up a website

I dislike how this article handwaves its own recommendation away. The steps required to “have a fucking website” are so much more complex than they used to be. Mandatory† TLS is the biggest hurdle, because now there needs to be software running to renew your certs instead of just tossing some plain files up in a directory on an HTTP server that could run for years unattended. Gone are the days when it was easy for a website to outlive its author, and it's our fault!

† Yes, the fact that the world's most popular browser puts a big red NOT SECURE!!! warning next to any non-TLS website makes it mandatory regardless of the fact that plain HTTP still technically loads. Scareware works on people or they wouldn't do it.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately highlights modern technical challenges that complicate website maintenance, especially for non-experts.

comments.author: treenode
comments.text: Fun rant to read, but this is an entitled view. Not everyone has to have a website, or has to care about democratising the internet. If you don't want to do business with them just because you shun platforms, that's up to you. They may be doing just fine without your patronage.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a reasonable perspective that having a website is not universally necessary, reflecting diverse business strategies.

comments.author: askmike
comments.text:
I think most comments miss the point on why many small businesses don't have websites:

It's not about it being hard to create and manage a website, it's that the vast majority of customers use social media platforms (as well as platforms like google maps) to find out about shops and F&B. For many businesses having an Instagram page will draw a lot more people than having a random website.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly points out customer behavior trends that reduce the incentive for small businesses to maintain standalone websites.

comments.author: Paulo75
comments.text: This is the kind of thing that feels obvious but apparently still needs to be said. I've seen businesses run entirely off an Instagram page, and when the algorithm changes or the account gets flagged, they lose everything overnight. No way to reach their customers, no archive of their work, nothing.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately warns about the risks of relying exclusively on third-party platforms for business presence.

comments.author: ifh-hn
comments.text:
Easier said than done, and completely ignoring the intricacies of "just have a website".

I can write the html, CSS, JavaScript needed for a website, I can spin up a local web server to serve these files, but setting up an internet facing website, no. No clue how to go about it, how to secure it, and how to maintain it.

Give me a step by step guide that is simple, and can ensure security and privacy, and I'll have a website. But until then I'll use what's convenient.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a common gap between technical skills and practical deployment knowledge, underscoring a real barrier.

comments.author: zjp
comments.text: Millennials delenda est. Or maybe Gen X. But definitely millennials. I am stockpiling champagne for when performative profanity goes to the grave with the silent generation against which it is still rebelling 70 years later. I do not want to order the sloppy toppy burger at BURGERSLUT. Just give me a cheeseburger. But yes, you should build a website.
comments.analysis: This comment is more emotional and less substantive, mixing cultural commentary with agreement on having a website.

comments.author: temp0826
comments.text: Unnecessary vulgarity makes you look stupid.
comments.analysis: This comment is a brief emotional critique of tone without contributing to the discussion on websites.
